item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the best excellent examples of fridge horror I've ever come across. How this article distributes information, sets up red herrings, and reveals its twist is nothing short of masterful.

item.title: My Speculation
item.content:encoded:
I'll try my best to convey my ideas on what SCP-3980 could be. (I'll probably edit this post if I come up with anything else or want to change something.)

+ My speculation
- My speculation

Some of my ideas I think may have come from other people.

I believe that SCP-3980 is an infohazardous anomaly that transfers between people and interferes with people's concepts of what's "alive" and what's "dead."

For the list of traits, there are 3 that I think are true (or true to some extent), and they are:
1. SCP-3980 is infectious, being transmissible between human subjects (Researcher Bond)
2. SCP-3980 requires either a willful human operator/host (D-774), or conspirator (D-209) to enter an active state
3. SCP-3980 exists in a purely metaphysical sense and is a potent informational hazard - full cognizance of SCP-3980 is sufficient to kill subjects (Director Kim)

For point #1, during the interview, the interviewer starts out alive, but later 'dies' once Director Kim states three words.

For point #2, SCP-3980 (I think) activates whenever it's being talked about (and maybe with). Again during the interview, SCP-3980 seems to activate.

For point #3, SCP-3980 being a potent infohazard and having full cognizance of SCP-3980 being sufficient to 'kill' subjects is probably what activates SCP-3980's ability to interfere with the "alive" and "dead" concepts. When Director Kim says the 'three words' in the interview, the interviewer 'dies' shortly after.

The reason why I think SCP-3980 switches the 'alive' and 'dead' concepts is the fact that the supposedly 'dead' interviewer needs 4 guards to be restrained and why Director Kim maybe is two entities and referred with the 'they' pronouns is because the interviewer is referred to as a 'they' then as a 'he'.

Hopefully, Shaggydredlocks can confirm (if he's willing) what I got right and/or wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What are the December 15th edits that add stuff to the source that you can't see in the front-facing article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 2. Director Kim could die between Incident LOCKE/2980 and the interview, not exactly during Incident.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Possibly because they started getting transmissions when survirors burried at FOB Locke started dying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I've got it:

The corpses produce a memetic effect that make people think they're alive, and when someone breaks that memetic effect, it spreads to them and makes people think they're dead. Might be wrong, it's an odd skip for sure.

item.title: Okay, then...
item.content:encoded:
Really late to the party here.

So, okay, it's pretty clear that it's a memetic cognitohazard such that when you're infected with it, others think you're dead when you're alive, and you're alive when you're dead.

This is clear from the treatment of Kim, who is being treated as alive even though he's dead, and the statement that there were no survivors on-site, even though there are transmissions to ignore. Plus the conversion of the interviewer to a "dead" person who needs four people to be dragged away.

+ show block
– hide block

It even fits with the contradictory statements, at least some of them. "There may be as few as five (Doctor Peterson) or as many as ten thousand instances (Private Awde) of SCP-3980." There's a handful of dead instances who seem alive, and a lot of live instances who seem dead, though 10,000 is a lot more than the 107 that's thought to have died at the site.

I think people are getting a little obsessed over "them". It's also used as a gender-neutral pronoun, guys. If on one level you think Kim can talk (alive) but on another level you realize Kim can't (dead), I could see using "them," or that could even be Kim's preferred pronouns for all we know.

And really, most of the other statements fit this "simple" profile of the dead-alive meme thing.

It's self-replicating, sure, because it's a meme. And self-sustaining, for similar reasons, and because it's a meme that can spread via hallucinations caused by dead people. It's ectoentropic because it makes people dead because of their mental state, tho that's really sort of an illusion, which fits with it being "possibly" ectoentropic.

It's transmissible between human subjects because it's a meme. Note that the interviewer doesn't "catch" it until the Kim-hallucination explains it to him. I kinda feel these hallucinations aren't just from the heads of the people viewing them, but share something with SCP-2428 (Mr. Mad), in that they kinda have some of their own reality. I think they kinda act like their living counterparts might, to some degree, in part to allow spread of the meme.

On that note, the meme being a space-time aberration as well would fit with its ability to produce sapient hallucinations. I also have to wonder if there's some sort of time loop going on here, but info is pretty thin, so… shrug

"SCP-3980 exists in a purely metaphysical sense and is a potent informational hazard - full cognizance of SCP-3980 is sufficient to kill subjects" is kinda true. If you're alive and infects you, everyone thinks you're dead. And if you end up in an autopsy, you're going to end up dead really, really soon.

"SCP-3980 requires either a willful human operator/host (D-774) or conspirator (D-209)". This seems like a difficult meme to spread unless you do it on purpose.

No idea what's up with the size things. Something about the size of a neuron and/or 1 AU? I think I have the wrong background for the guesses here.

So this is what I think happened. The people who were off-site got infected, but were alive. I suspect if you're infected, you're not affected… They all knew they were alive. But they come back to the site, and everyone treated them as dead. In fact, I suspect that since non-infected can tell "dead" people need to be restrained, this looks like a zombie plague.

This explains the rest. The team or someone on it — probably Kim — decides the only way to fix this is to infect everyone, doing it over an intercom or something. Suddenly you have something that looks, to the outside, like a BIG zombie plague. This leads to the razing of the surface level and entombing the survivors in concrete.

There are some weird details here. Part of me wants to suspect Kim or part/all of the team died (killed by people reacting to them as zombies) and then it was the hallucinations that infected everyone else, or they died in the fight that razed the surface level to the ground, or that the meme always kills you eventually, so it can create hallucinations that serve as infection vectors.

I think that's why it says "The guilty party is to be executed; the method of termination has yet to be decided." Non-infected think the living people are zombies, so they can't really execute them, so they need to figure out a novel method of termination.

The only thing that doesn't 100% fit with the idea is the non-infected belief the infected corpses are alive and not zombies. But maybe they think they're immortal in a different way? I mean, they're unkillable. You shoot Kim, and he remains "alive" as far as those infected are concerned.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

Again, details are fuzzy. But I think that's the general gist of things.

-Kirt

P.S. Antimemetic tag is because I think you have to be infected or else you forget how it works and/or can't really figure it out.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Bit late to come back to the party, but I'm still on the second page, so it looks like the ideas are still mine!

But basically...
Hide

I think I reached a similar conclusion as Photosynthetic later in the thread, except that I don't think it switched the states of "alive" and "dead," but rather, everyone died, either in this event or before; the "living" in this case are those not yet affected by the anomaly, which, I believe, destroyed the idea of death.

That's what the quote is about; if we no longer associated corpses and all their smells and rottings and other sensory perceptions with the concept of "dead," then until we are told otherwise, we'd fail to recognize that they are dead. If we ourselves are also dead, then in acknowledging that death exists, we too would die, but to everyone else, we'd still be alive.

The sudden skin sloughs could therefore be explained away as the interviewer finally recognizing what 14 years of decay would look like on a dead body.

Hide

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was missing the Keter tag, so I added it in. If this was in error, please revert.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ I think we’ve missed an important point.
– hide block

I agree with several of the posters on page 1 of the comment thread: this anomaly screws with perception. Once you’re infected with it, everyone not affected perceives your life/death states to be inverted. You’ll be treated as “dead” for as long as you’re alive, and then as “alive” again after your death. Someone interacting with a “living” corpse will subconsciously fill in actions and conversations that they consider likely — Kim’s dialogue isn’t transcribed because he didn’t actually say anything, you just thought he did while watching the video. Infection requires only that you recognize the anomaly for what it is: the interviewer was infected when he perceived Kim yelling “I AM DEAD.” (Hell of a way for your subconscious to force a conscious realization.)

But this doesn’t quite explain everything:

  • Why didn’t the Foundation notice Kim, and all other off-site FOB Locke personnel, spontaneously “dropping dead” at the moment this thing triggered?
  • Why is the note about ignoring all transmissions still there, 14 years later? Surely the base didn’t have enough food, water, air, etc. to keep its personnel going for that long, did it? That seems unnecessarily overprepared even for the Foundation.

I think it’s because, for the people affected when it first activated, this thing didn’t just switch perceived life/death states, but also actual ones. At the moment of activation, all the off-base personnel simultaneously acquired the perceptual effect and actually died. Since it happened all at once, nobody had time to notice the difference.

If that’s the case, what happens when that Interviewer actually dies? Will the Foundation see him “resurrected” and freak out? Maybe he just can’t die now — the anomaly’s effects are permanent. (That’s the nature of death, after all. It’s irreversible.) I don’t envy him the autopsy.

That permanence would also explain the continuing transmissions from FOB Locke. They don’t need fourteen years of food, water, and air to keep them alive. They’ve been entombed in concrete — they should be dead — but the anomaly swapped those states. It’s keeping them alive, and there’s no reason to think it’ll ever stop.

item.title: Community Outreach Team Captain Post
item.content:encoded: As the author has been disqualified for poor sportsmanship, this entry has been retroactively removed from the Jam Contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get it.

I don't get it.

+1.

I just thought this number should be remembered here.

Still trying to figure this out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted this for the inherent mind-screw nature. I wish I could re-upvote after reading the comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man this makes my head spin. I really like it but I'm pretty lost as to how to solve the riddle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is simply excellent. It is a good mystery for the reader to solve, well executed, and effectively works as a refreshing take on the SCP format. Upvoted, already.

+ Now, my take on it
- This can take some time...

Lets begin with the tittle itself, "Blind lead the blind". What I can get from this immediately is that no one really knows what is happening in the description of the skip. It doesn't means that the in-universe author is blind about it, but every personnel trying to figure it out in the description is taking cues from someone equally unable to know.

Object Class Keter (Provisional). It is a very strange way of putting it. This is actually on its way to containment. That says two things to us: first, that the author actually recognizes the current state of the skip as being extremely hard to contain (it isn't stated at no point as uncontained), second that this state is expected to change soon, more reliable containment is already on the way. That means that the author already knows the answer to the mystery, and how to contain the skip. My guess is that the archive is itself a part of the real Containment Procedures for this skip, and the current one, by the way, since it isn't Euclid yet.

Starting the Procedures we get a lot more. FOB Locke is the actual containment site. By use of the expression De Facto, we can wonder if they pretend to contain it on a De Jure containment site. We can assume that the things are happening actually at FOB Locke without personnel realizing it, but maybe I'm stretching things here. We also learn that the site was razed above ground and entombed, but not obstructed. That is, it is currently a subterranean structure with no access to the surface, but is still there. Finally, that this state of things is considered enough physical containment, what gives us two more facts, one that this skip does need a physical containment (not necessarily that it have a physical presence, but that it have a spatial presence of sorts), and it already have enough of it, but also that it requires a non-physical containment, and we are not assured on this. So it is the informational containment that lacks reliability (for now).

I can't shake the feeling that the expression "there were no survivors" is both an important containment element and a relatively minor part of this. Maybe it reinforces the logic necessary for the containment to work, but above all it reinforces the previous and more relevant line. We now that there ARE transmissions from FOB Locke, but also that they must be ignored for the containment to work properly. Since the transmissions are not mentioned again in the description, they are probably non-anomalous, so I guess for now that the important element is to ignore the entities still living in FOB Locke. IF I'm correct about the transmissions being non-anomalous themselves, we can assume that FOB-Locke still have both entities with the capacity to operate radio and devices still in operation conditions. I won't discard yet the possibility of FOB Locke being still manned by non-anomalous and non-affected personnel.

Personnel from FOB Locke detained is no more personnel from FOB Locke. It means that the people presented either aren't more in FOB Locke, or aren't more personnel. They are either corpses or SCP, since even D-Classes are referred as personnel. Also we got a subtle possibility here. FOB Locke is referred as former, so it does have another designation now, and it is a containment site. It can just be site-87. If this is so, we know for sure that the "former personnel" described aren't Foundation personnel in any capacity anymore. If site-87 is actually another site, then those people are neither SCP nor corpses being affected, because in those cases they would belong on the actual containment site, that is FOB Locke. My guess is that 87 is FOB, but for now it can all be just misleading information.

We get to the description, where the author supposedly tries to say what is going on, not anymore the "how to keep this contained". So in theory, information here isn't directly relevant to contain this thing anymore, but for you to understand what is being contained. Since this archive is also a containment implement, this information does have a double purpose by nature. It starts by telling us that the skip is responsible for the loss of the very containment site that currently contains it well enough, that wasn't even called a site then. Maybe the Event wasn't just a manifestation of the skip, but its very containment process. We are also told that 107 Foundation personnel died, and that it happened on 14 February 2000. Observe that the line about recent acquisition isn't about FOB Locke, but about the skip itself, that means the Foundation had it just prior to the Event. It is emphasized that ascertaining the nature and properties of the skip with precision wasn't possible due to it being recently acquired, the Event being too destructive, and physical documentation being lost along with all hands on site. It doesn't says, though, that those things are connected, and in fact the way it is stated makes me think that the Event isn't responsible for the loss of workforce nor the loss of physical documentation. Lets look at what it doesn't tell us. It doesn't tell us that those 107 deaths where all the workforce on site, or that all workforce lost was lost to death. It doesn't tell us if there was electronic documentation of quality, though it does tell us that it wouldn't be enough to give us exact answers about the skip. It doesn't tell us, by the way, how good is the data we have on the skip, just that it isn't enough for exact answers (and we have exact answers to very few if any skip, anyway). It doesn't says how the skip is responsible for the Event, so we can't derive direct information about one from the other. And it doesn't says much of what the Event actually destroyed, besides the surface of FOB Locke and 107 lives, just that it had a destructive nature that is partially responsible for a degree of lack of data on the skip.

Given the time of the night, I'll let it rest for now and take to it again tomorrow.

- This can take some time...

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This skip is fucking cool. Presumably, the sun went red and killed them all :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quite the mind-bender here. Not entirely sure what the solution is, but it definitely sounds like a "patient zero is all that's needed" kind of memetic infection. In short, I would agree that the reason why the SCP file explicitly states "there were no survivors" is because 1) those infected by SCP-3980 can generate an indistinguishable hallucination of a live body, and 2) if you believe someone survived the incident, then if you end up conversing with an infected corpse, you'll become one, too.

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I win.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This made me feel stupid, but intrigued. I'll admit I had to go to the chat to understand what on Earth I had just read. This probably isn't for me, but when you have an SCP that spawns theories, and headcanons. You've done something right.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First,this is a very interesting article that I will give upvote.
As I have discussed with my friend, some people think SCP-3980 can make people reversal dead and alive, they think kim is already dead. I do not think so, here is my idea:
1.SCP-3980 is some kind of creature that eat people, and kim was eaten in 2000, what did the interviewer see is SCP-3980, which imitate kim to enter scp-foundation.
2.So why I think this kim is not the real one. Kim was found in 2000, and this interview was hold in 2004. During this 4 years, kim was treated alive, but not a corpse. As a medical student ,I know that a corpse cannot keep fresh after 4 years. So this kim is some other thing, which I think is SCP-3980.
3.Some people think the people in FOB Locke were alive. I think they are dead. The transmissions were released by SCP-3980, to entice people enter FOB Locke, and eat them.
4.If this interview was recorded by a video, why we cannot see "kim:"? Cause kim cannot speak, SCP-3980 cannot speak.
5.I think SCP-3980 can only let people treat alive people as a dead one.Like the interviewer. After he is "dead", SCP-3980 can eat him as a corpse, and no one will notice that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one confused the hell out of me, although I think that's more because I'm easily confused and less of the article actually being confusing. It took reading through some of the comments to get it to really click in my head. Good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good. It gets you hooked on figuring out what's going on, and all the small details that you notice after enough rereads help bolster this. I got so into this, that I originally had a block of text here that detailed what I thought it was after reading the other theories and comments, before deleting it because no one cared and it took up space. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thrown off by the list of traits from the researchers, thinking this was a more traditional logic puzzle. Reading the rest of the theorizing from everyone else gave me the "ah HA!" moment. This manages to just skirt the edge of being too clever for it's own good, but the revelation has a lot of impact. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "'alive' FOB Locke personnel are dead and 'dead' FOB Locke personnel are alive" thing seems correct, but there are still questions left unanswered (or which just aren't obvious to my dolt brain).

Like, assuming that SCP-3980 is tied to the body (i.e. that all affected corpses are seen as living breathing people by everyone), does it only affect the bodies of people who worked at FOB Locke for some reason? If so, why are all off-site staff dead? I could only assume it's deceased and buried former staff that the Foundation dug up, but "all off-site staff were found buried in coffins with corresponding obituaries and death certificates" is an odd thing to be left out of a report.

If I'm assuming correctly, why do SCP-3980 containment staff have to be told to disregard transmissions, if they'll be unable to perceive the "dead" staff as survivors anyway?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see everyone is scratching their heads, so if that was your goal, congrats

+ Headcanon
- Close

Infohazard that kills and causes, as many have already said, the dead to appear alive and vice versa. The flies swarming the interviewer were non-anomalous flies attracted to the interviewer, who was affected, dead and decomposing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

and also the title "Blind Lead the Blind"
means all statements form the former FOB Locke personnel is not important.

and form "Director Kim shouts animatedly at the interviewer, redirecting their attention. They state three words.", actually the interviewer states these tree words.could be "you are dead"

he finally reachs the truth, and become the next victim.

"he" and "they" is the only unsolved problem remains.

how about this:3980 is an entity, information about 3980 is antimemetic, like 055 and 3125.
3980 stays with kim , but transfers to the interviewer. so kim becomes "him" ,and can "shouts" rather than "shout". the interviewer becomes "they".after affected the interviewer, 3980 transfers back to kim.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the tags reveal a lot….

someone from cn-branch used a simily trick recently

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
upvoted.

Me and my friends' guesses...
Not sure if they are correct

Director Kim is lead in by two armed guards. They are placed in their seat.

And Kim is hereinafter referred to as "they". There must be a reason why the personal pronoun changed. I guess that SCP-3980 is a group instance that can "stay" in the body of an infectee, or it can walk around without being noticed. The Foundation can realise that Kim was not alone during the interview, or "they" actually refer to another group of instances.

Such assumption is confirmed by this sentence.

The guilty party is to be executed, the method of termination is currently undecided.

I guess that the "guilty party" actually means "the host of SCP-3980". If this is true, they are trying to find out the host so SCP-3980 can be neutralized.

Director Kim details their remembrance of the anomaly to the best of their knowledge. As they do, the interviewer becomes preoccupied with the killing of several more flies.

Well.. one fly, and then more flies. Surely the flies didn't come out from thin air, so I guess their presence are not noticed before. So when the interviewer obtain more and more knowledge about the anomaly, he was gradually aware that Kim was actually a corpse. That is why he became more and more annoyed by the flies, which, he failed to notice before.(Could be otherwise, i.e. when the interviewer notice the flies, he became aware of the anomaly.)

Also, it is a bit strange that an interviewer would stop talking with the interviewee and kill some flies. Is it possible that, the flies are not died, but they are affected by SCP-3980 and are considered died? "becomes preoccupied with the killing of several more flies." the flies might not be killed by the interviewer at all. He was observing the flies being converted and realise the true anomaly of 3980.

Director Kim shouts animatedly at the interviewer, redirecting their attention.

And when Kim shouted, the personal pronoun for the interviewer changed! That is to say, if I guess correctly, SCP-3980 temporary left Kim's body and went into that of the interviewer at this moment, and went back shortly afterwards. This made the interviewer infected.

They state three words.

I might be wrong about this, but.. who stated the three words? Since what Kim "said" is actually what others believe it would say, if Kim stated these words(I am dead?), then only the interviewer knows it. Is it possible that the interviewer state three words(You are dead?)? Or SCP-3980 itself said that?

I'm also wondering if the Foundation has already figured out what the anomaly is. That's why they blocked all transmissions from FOB Locke and seal the survivors under concrete. Also, they take the interviewer away immediately for "postmortem examination", which will certainly kill him (and perhaps they know it), in order to prevent something (further spread of SCP-3980, I think).

Then.. After Incident LOCKE/3980 happens, some alive person were considered "dead" by others. They were transported out of the site, and buried outside. They died afterwards, and their corpses was again considered "alive". They were discovered by the Foundation, and taken to Site-087 as "survivors" (That is to say, that "they had been off-site at the time of the event" is not true. This is what others think they would "say"). SCP-3980 follows one of them into Site-087. SCP-3980 also managed to infect the remaining survivors in FOB Locke, so they are too considered dead by the Fondation.

item.title: My guesses
item.content:encoded:
+ Is the culprit...
- Doctor Kim?

"SCP-3980 requires either a willful human operator, host, or conspirator to enter an active state (Director Kim, D-209)"

This is the only statement that comes from Director Kim. So perhaps Kim has a knowledge/control over 3980 that others do not? That wouldn't explain the contradictions between this statement and the others. Still, if we assume this one to be true, than that would mean Kim is the willful operator, as they are the one who makes 3980 active towards the interviewer. Perhaps Kim is also D-209/ D-209 works with Kim?

+ The method of spearding 3980 is...
- telling someone who the culprit is

This would explain "Despite this, all personnel are in agreement that Incident LOCKE/3980 is the result of sabotage on the part of one of their number. Each suspect claims to know who this saboteur is, but are incapable of providing this information when prompted; the suspects are otherwise wholly compliant with their imprisonment." I'm certain that revealing the culprit and spreading the SCP are related in some way. Even if all the information about 3980 is false and being made up by the cognitive effects of 3980 making people think corpses are alive, there's no reason that they wouldn't be able to, falsely, reveal who the culprit is. Otherwise, we should also have contradictory information about who the culprit is.

These are just my guesses and might contradict with what others have said. Regardless, I really enjoyed this! It reminds me of those old detective books where you'd have a mystery a couple pages long, which I loved as a kid

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was almost entirely lost throughout this whole thing, but having read over people's explanations and re-reading the article itself, it's clear that there's plenty of info here to solve the mystery. I'm just no good at whodunits.

A well-deserved +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the time I read this the score jumped up by about five points, and I can see why. Of all the murder mysteries presented in this portion of the contest (that I've read so far), this one felt like it provided the best mystery to solve, but all the solutions that people are coming up with are really great.

Solid work shaggy.

+1.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Not quite sure what you're getting at, but it's cool how many ways there are to read into this! :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well damn.

This is pretty frikkin' great.

Easy +1

Solid piece Shaggy. Something dun eff'ed up, and that generally does make for a good sort of story. In this case, we don't even know what that -something- is, and that leads to all sorts of solid discussion.

To which, I'd like to note...
Hide

The initial incident took place on 2/14/2000.

The interview is conducted on 8/1/2014.

To quote directly from Wikipedia, John Locke "maintained that we are born without innate ideas, and that knowledge is instead determined only by experience derived from sense perception."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congrats! You get a cookie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But if this theory is right...
– hide block

…then all of the stuff from the bullet points is a red herring. After all, this information all came from people who are actually dead, so it's not likely they are actually talking. Like OptimisticLucio said, it's probably the people around them "filling in" what they think they might say, which would explain why the different descriptions are so contradictory.

What I really want to know is, what happened to all the people who weren't at the facility?

+ show block
– hide block


Why are they all dead?

item.title: Re: Tough Nut to Crack
item.content:encoded: Darn it! I'm so lost

item.title: Re: Tough Nut to Crack
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry, but you're off-base with thinking people have merged. The commas/slashes are meant to indicate which personnel share each opinion, but it could use a tiny bit of cleaning post-contest.

Glad you're having fun with the mystery!

item.title: Tough Nut to Crack
item.content:encoded:
So first off, I can't stop thinking about this one. Nice work, +1.

Although I still haven't figured this out, I believe I have at least one new piece of the puzzle. Maybe someone can put all this together:

+ A valuable datapoint?
- or maybe not

There is definitely some sort of merging of personnel going on in addition to the dead/alive stuff. In the interview, Dr. Kim is referred to as "they" and "their". That reads very odd, and at first I thought it was a reference to him being a corpse and no longer being a he/she, but rather an it. But again, you would say "it", not "they", for singular.

Then I went back and reread (again!), and I found this nugget:
There may be as few as five, or as many as ten thousand instances of SCP-3980 (Doctor Walters, et al/Private Awde)

What's the et al doing in there, when we are talking about a list of specific people? And why are some names separated by commas, and some by slashes? What's the difference? My theory is that somehow multiple possibly dead/possibly alive people have merged, and the SCP makes it so no one unaffected thinks that is strange at all.

Final point of interest, which is probably a real stretch, but I'm desperate:
SCP-3980 requires either a willful human operator, host, or conspirator to enter an active state (Director Kim, D-209)

What is a D-class person doing in a list of "former FOB Locke personnel", and what was he doing offsite the day before?

Hope someone can make all this work somehow, I'm at my brain power and time limit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My favorite entry for Day 1. A nicely done logic puzzle that fits quite a bit into a small amount of space.

+1. This is a bit similar to the collab we were doing a while back, but all the elements from that that you're using in this draft were pretty much all the ones you come up with yourself, so I don't care.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And then Interviewer was a corpse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All I can say is that I negelected to rectify a formatting quirk with the interview. It should be a video file, not a transcript.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Possibly relevant to determining what's going on, possibly not:

+ show block
– hide block

The former Forward Operating Base Locke has become the de-facto containment site for SCP-3980. The above-ground portions of the base have been razed; the subterranean areas have been entombed beneath several thousand tons of concrete, and paved over.

Currently, all information on SCP-3980 has been gleaned through the first-hand accounts of the personnel who were stationed at FOB Locke, and had been off-site at the time of the event

Director Kim slouches further in their seat, the flesh around their lips can be seen sloughing off. They begin to recount their final day on-site, one day prior to the incident.

The interviewer is lead to the Site morgue for postmortem examination.

Maybe the solution involves figuring out what site this interview is taking place at?

Possible Solution
– hide block

Were the three words "You are SCP-3980"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, well if you fix it I'll try not to cheat by compare contrasting!

(We are actually missing further points here, right?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think, with that change, I see what's going on. Well, at least in the interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Semi-tentative +1!

FEEDBACK
– hide block

I don't know what's going on, but I get the sense that you do — which is the most important thing.

The parts I do understand are really strong, and really good; the first clue we get that something is wrong is in the special containment procedures ("All transmissions from FOB Locke are to be disregarded. There were no survivors."), and it's a neat banger — especially at the end of the final interview, when we realize what this line (in addition to the rest of the containment procedures) actually means.

(I also like how 'There were no survivors' sounds just clinical enough to pass, but just unclinical enough to draw our attention to it; it's a subtle cue that something's gone wrong, and we should pay attention)

The interview is what got me to give this a +1 — that being said, there's a few things here that I feel are holding the article back:

The flies and 'lips sloughing off' were, in my mind, like an anvil hitting the reader on the head. I don't think you need them, and it makes the interview all the more chilling without them. The final line ('Four guards were necessary to restrain him') almost directly tells us what's going on, and contextualizes Kim's unusual 'method' of 'disclosing' details.

If you feel like you still need something to convince us that Kim is dead, you could possibly have the interview gag from the smell during the interview.

There's also a couple of grammar nits I have, but they're all fairly minor:

The guilty party is to be executed, the method of termination is currently undecided.

Should be:

The guilty party is to be executed; the method of termination has yet to be decided.

SCP-3980 is an unknown object or entity responsible for the loss of FOB Locke and the deaths of 107 Foundation personnel on 2/14/2000, an event designated Incident LOCKE/3980.

Should (probably) be:

SCP-3980 is an unidentified anomaly responsible for the loss of FOB Locke and the deaths of 107 Foundation personnel on 2/14/2000; this event has been designated Incident LOCKE/3980.

Due to its recent acquiry prior to this date, the destructive nature of the event in question, and the loss of physical documentation as well as all hands on-site; its exact nature and properties have yet to be definitively ascertained.

That last highlighted semi-colon is incorrect — it should be a comma. That being said, making it a comma also makes this sentence unwieldy. After the contest, I'd suggest trying to rewrite this bit a little.

The following is a summarized list of traits have been ascribed to SCP-3980 by former FOB Locke personnel:

Should be:

The following is a summarized list of traits that have been ascribed to SCP-3980 by former FOB Locke personnel:

Good luck with the contest!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mfw people are getting closer and closer to solving it, before I realize that a minor hiccup in formatting obscures a particular important element. Apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+We're on the right track, but...
My thoughts

The dead/alive swap is likely correct, but there's still unanswered questions. For starters, some of the traits in the bullet points don't line up with that effect: why would it have a size (which are one dimensional units for that matter), why would it be a "space-time aberration", and how would there be "instances" of the SCP if the SCP is the memetic effect itself and not its infectees? Are some of the "surviving" researchers lying or mistaken? Doctor Walters claimed it was ectoentropic, had particular instances and was a space time aberration, none of which makes any sense with the dead/alive swap effect.

Why can't/won't any of them disclose the traitor? What did the traitor do in the first place?

There's a weird line in the containment procedures, "The guilty party is to be executed, the method of termination is currently undecided". The termination procedure is unimportant for a supposedly alive non-anomalous person, so I think the author of the document is aware of the effect and is unsure how to actually "execute" a dead person!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed that issue with their gender, it's a leftover from pumping this out real quick-like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it. o.O Is this supposed to be a logic puzzle or something? It started off interesting, but the interview is just confusing. Are you supposed to have switched Dr. Kim's gender in the middle?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
EDIT 2: Let’s turn this comment into something useful

So.. if I got this right..

+ Summary of what I understand
- It’s probably wrong

So, SCP-3980 is a contagious Infohazard, that makes everyone who is both alive and ‘infected’ by it appear dead to all outside viewers, and anyone dead and ‘infected’ appear alive. Any conversations with dead 3980-1 instances is just the speaker’s head filling in words (The corpse isn’t actually saying anything, but the person ‘talking’ to it hears what they thing the corpse would say if they were.. not a corpse). The vector of transmission is someone realizing that the people infected by 3980 are alive/dead, despite appearing dead/alive (The three words that Kim ‘says’ in the interview are ‘I am dead’), which explains why the Foundation explicitly states “there were no survivors”, and seemingly unnecessarily bury the entire site in cement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
everyone rn

I think I get it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can I guess again?
My new and improved theory:

Whatever SCP-3980 is is possessing the staff members, that's obvious. But the title is, The Blind Lead the Blind. Is the title referring to us, being led by the characters, or something else? SCP-3980 could be killing the people to gain a body of its/their own so they can interact with the outside world. Also, we can assume everybody is lying, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would make sense...
I think.

If that's the case then there's 107 members of staff trapped under several thousand tons of concrete.

All transmissions from FOB Locke are to be disregarded. There were no survivors.

I'm convinced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I've got this.
Or at least part of it.

Firstly, Kim is dead. That's why there's flies everywhere, and the whole "sloughing flesh" thing, and why they have to be "dragged" to their quarters. Part of the anomaly is that you see dead people as alive.

Secondly, the interviewer is still alive. This is more obvious than Kim, given the whole "four guards" bit, and suggests that the anomaly somehow makes dead people seem alive and alive people be seen as dead. A classic Shaggy dichotomy-switcheroo.

I'm stuck from there, but maybe someone smarter than me can use this to piece together a little more of this nightmare.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anomalous malaria. They're mosquitoes, not flies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can absolutely guarantee there's enough here to get it, in fact, I feared that I revealed too much, so this is reassuring. Best of luck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just feels like it's a creepy, confusing mess that doesn't lead much of anywhere. Like, in your head you can think "Oh, maybe it's the flies, or maybe it's the guards, or maybe nothing's happening at all" but I feel as though it's just confusing for confusion's sake. It would do much better to be expanded on, I'd say, but until then I can't really upvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, I was actually pretty geared up for a proper whodunnit before creepyweird happened. But creepyweird is the reason I come here, so +1.
